Details:
Bench-scale studies suggest that removal of lithium from water by adsorptive media can be effective depending on the type of media. One study [2736] using an iron-based media achieved a maximum of greater than 99 percent removal of lithium. Two studies evaluated the effectiveness of manganese dioxide-based medias, showing up to 90 [2739] and greater than 90 [2740] percent removal. One study [2737] evaluated the feasibility of using fabricated titanate nanotubes to remove lithium and reported 63 to 80 percent removal. Another study [2741] testing the effectiveness of two zeolitic rocks for removal of lithium found clinoptilolite to be up to 67 percent effective and laumontite to be up to 33 percent effective.

A study [2742] using date pits as an adsorptive media displayed varying removal rates depending on the addition of an ionic liquid and cellulose. Results suggested that the vast majority of removal was accomplished by the addition of the ionic liquid and cellulose, as date pits without the addition achieved only 18 to 45 percent removal, whereas the same media with the addition of the ionic solution and cellulose achieved 55 to 90 percent removal.
